condensed_summary: Docebo AgentHub straddles three large, fast-growing software markets: corporate LMS/learning (~USD 14.5-16.3B in 2025, ~20-23% CAGR), enterprise knowledge management software (~USD 13.7-26.4B in 2025, ~14-18% CAGR), and skills intelligence/talent marketplace (~USD 9.6-9.8B in 2024, ~11-19% CAGR), giving a combined adjacent TAM roughly in the USD 40-50B range. The individual markets are in a Growth stage, but the converged 'learning + knowledge + skills with agentic AI' category AgentHub is defining is Emerging and not yet sized by analysts, so the immediate SAM is undefined. The opportunity window is driven by a specific, accelerating macro shift: enterprise agentic AI moving from <5% of enterprise apps in 2025 to a forecast 40% with task-specific AI agents by 2026 (Gartner), colliding with documented knowledge fragmentation (employees lose ~1.8-3.6 hours/day searching across 5+ disconnected systems) and the rise of skills-based organizations. Validation signals are strong: Glean (enterprise AI search/agents) hit ~USD 300M ARR by May 2026 at a USD 7.2B valuation, 61% of orgs have adopted or are testing AI in L&D, and Docebo's own ARR growth reaccelerated to 10.6% in Q1 2026. The main risk is that the category is unproven and AgentHub's agents are not yet live (fall 2026), so the window favors fast category definition over a defensible installed base.

market_size.tam: Combined adjacent TAM ~USD 40-50B (2025-2026): Corporate LMS/learning ~USD 14.49-16.33B (2025); Enterprise Knowledge Management software ~USD 13.70-26.4B (2025); Skills intelligence / talent marketplace ~USD 9.6-9.8B (2024). The converged 'learning + knowledge + skills with agentic AI' category that AgentHub defines is not yet sized by analysts.
market_size.tam_source: Precedence Research / Research and Markets (corporate LMS, https://www.precedenceresearch.com/corporate-learning-management-system-market); Fortune Business Insights / Mordor Intelligence (KM software, https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/knowledge-management-software-market-110376); Verified Market Reports / OpenPR (talent marketplace & talent intelligence, https://www.verifiedmarketreports.com/product/talent-marketplace-platform-market/)
market_size.sam: Not reliably quantifiable. A directional SAM is the mid-market-to-enterprise (500+ employee) segment of the corporate LMS market in North America and EMEA, which represents the majority of the ~USD 14.5-16.3B LMS market; however no analyst breaks out a SAM for the converged agentic-AI learning+knowledge+skills category, which does not yet exist as a tracked segment.
market_size.discrepancy_detail: Sources diverge by more than 30% on the enterprise knowledge management market. Estimates for 2025 range from ~USD 13.70B (KM software, Mordor) to ~USD 23.2B (KM software, alternate source) to ~USD 26.4B (2026, alternate source); one outlier defines a broad 'knowledge management' market at ~USD 961B (likely including services/consulting). The software-only KM market is best read as ~USD 13.7-26.4B with ~14-18% CAGR. The corporate LMS market also shows >30% spread in 2025 size (~USD 14.49B Precedence vs ~USD 16.33B Research and Markets) and CAGR (19.65% to 22.9%). Both figures presented rather than a single point estimate.

growth_rate.rate: Corporate LMS ~20-23% CAGR (mid-point ~22%); Enterprise KM software ~14-18% CAGR (mid-point ~17.7%); Skills intelligence / talent intelligence ~11-19% CAGR (talent marketplace ~10.9%, talent intelligence ~18.7%). Underlying agentic AI agents market ~44-46% CAGR through 2030.
growth_rate.period: 2025/2026 base year through 2030-2034 depending on report
growth_rate.source: natlawreview.com/researchandmarkets (LMS 22.9% / 22.0%), Precedence Research (LMS 19.65% 2025-2034), Mordor/Fortune Business Insights (KM 17.7-18.5%), OpenPR/Verified Market Reports (talent intelligence 18.7%, talent marketplace 10.9%), Gartner / tech-insider.org (agentic AI agents 44-46% CAGR)

evidence: The three underlying markets are in clear Growth stage with double-digit CAGRs (LMS ~20-23%, KM ~14-18%, skills/talent intelligence ~11-19%) and large established incumbents (Cornerstone, SAP SuccessFactors, Workday, Notion, Guru, Gloat, Eightfold), per Precedence Research, Fortune Business Insights and OpenPR/Verified Market Reports.

Active consolidation and M&A signal a maturing core but a still-expanding frontier: Docebo acquired Zive (enterprise AI/knowledge) and 365Talents (skills intelligence, ~USD 54.6M, Jan 2026) to bundle learning + knowledge + skills, per businesswire.com and reworked.co.

Strong new-entrant and funding velocity at the AI-native edge: Glean reached ~USD 300M ARR (May 2026) at a USD 7.2B valuation after a USD 150M Series F, and launched agents powering 100M+ agent actions annually, per CNBC, Glean press and Sacra.

High and rising demand-side adoption: 61% of organizations have fully/partially adopted or are testing AI in L&D and 52% of L&D teams are increasing AI tool use in 2026, per Synthesia 'AI in L&D Report 2026' and TalentLMS 2026 L&D Report.

The specific converged category (learning + knowledge + skills + agentic AI) is itself Emerging/undefined - no analyst sizes it yet and AgentHub's agents are not live until fall 2026 - so the overall picture is a Growth market with an Emerging sub-category being created.

growth_signals.funding_activity: Heavy capital flowing into the adjacent enterprise-AI-knowledge space. Glean raised a USD 150M Series F at a USD 7.2B valuation (mid-2025), up from USD 4.6B nine months earlier, and hit ~USD 300M ARR by May 2026 (+89% YoY), per CNBC and Sacra. Docebo (NASDAQ/TSX: DCBO) is publicly traded and deployed M&A capital: 365Talents acquired for ~USD 54.6M (+ up to USD 5.1M earn-out) in Jan 2026 plus the Zive acquisition, per businesswire.com and reworked.co. Docebo's own ARR was USD 248.9M as of Q1 2026, up 10.6% YoY (an acceleration from 8.4% in FY2025), per Docebo Q1 2026 release. Skills intelligence and talent intelligence players (Gloat, Eightfold) continue to attract enterprise budgets.
growth_signals.job_posting_trends: Not directly quantified via job-board data in this research pass (gap). Indirect demand signal: HR professionals reportedly spend up to 30% of time searching across systems and only 34% of companies have a formal org-wide reskilling program, indicating large unmet operational need that typically drives L&D/knowledge tooling headcount, per Glean and 15five/imocha HR trends 2026.
growth_signals.new_entrant_rate: High at the AI-native frontier (Glean, plus a wave of enterprise AI search/agent startups noted by CNBC competing with Microsoft and Amazon) and active reconfiguration among incumbents via acquisition (Docebo/Zive/365Talents). The converged category specifically has very few direct entrants because Docebo is among the first to bundle all three layers with agentic AI, per businesswire.com and reworked.co.
growth_signals.search_interest: Direct Google Trends data not pulled in this pass (gap). Proxy demand signals are strong and current: Gartner forecasts 40% of enterprise apps will feature task-specific AI agents by 2026 (up from <5% in 2025) and agentic AI is the most aggressive adoption curve of any emerging tech in its survey, per Gartner newsroom.

gaps: No analyst sizes the specific converged 'learning + knowledge + skills with agentic AI' category, so AgentHub's true category-level TAM/SAM cannot be quantified - only the three adjacent markets can be summed, which overlaps and double-counts some demand.

Direct Google Trends search-interest data and quantified job-posting trend data were not pulled in this pass; growth-signal evidence relies on funding velocity, adoption surveys and analyst forecasts instead.
Knowledge management market estimates diverge >30% (and one outlier ~USD 961B likely includes services), so the KM TAM is presented as a range rather than a point estimate.

macro_shift.description: The convergence of enterprise agentic AI with knowledge fragmentation and the shift to skills-based organizations. Specifically: (1) task-specific AI agents are moving from <5% of enterprise applications in 2025 to a forecast 40% by 2026 (Gartner), pulling AI from chat into autonomous, in-workflow action; (2) employees lose an estimated 1.8-3.6 hours per day searching for information scattered across 5+ disconnected systems, creating acute demand to unify knowledge; and (3) skills-based hiring/workforce strategy is now mainstream (e.g., ~65% of employers adopting skills-based hiring for entry roles) while only 34% of companies have a formal org-wide reskilling program. AgentHub sits at the intersection of these three shifts.
macro_shift.evidence: Gartner: 40% of enterprise apps will feature task-specific AI agents by 2026, up from <5% in 2025; agentic AI is the most aggressive adoption curve of any emerging tech Gartner measured, with only ~17% of orgs having deployed agents but >60% expecting to within two years (Gartner newsroom, 2025-08-26). Knowledge fragmentation: employees spend ~1.8 hours/day (McKinsey) to ~3.6 hours/day searching for information, ~21% of work time searching plus ~14% recreating lost info, and 54% of organizations use more than 5 platforms for documenting/sharing information (Glean, Slite Enterprise Search Survey 2025, Bloomfire/HBR 2025). Skills-based org shift: ~65% of employers adopting skills-based hiring for entry-level roles, but only 34% have a formal org-wide reskilling program (NACE / imocha / 15five HR trends 2026). AI in L&D: 61% of orgs have adopted/are testing AI in L&D (Synthesia 2026). Market validation: Glean (enterprise AI search + agents) reached ~USD 300M ARR by May 2026 at a USD 7.2B valuation (CNBC, Sacra).
macro_shift.enterprise_adoption: Early-to-mid on the agentic-AI curve and accelerating fast. ~17% of organizations have actually deployed AI agents, but >60% expect to within two years and 43% are actively considering agentic AI in 2026 - the steepest adoption curve Gartner has measured. On the demand side, 61% of organizations have adopted or are testing AI in L&D and 52% are increasing AI tool use in 2026, but execution is uneven (security, accuracy, integration cited as top barriers by 36-58%). Large enterprises are past the experimentation gate and entering scaled deployment, which is precisely AgentHub's mid-market-to-enterprise (500+ employee) target.

findings.opportunity_connection: AgentHub is purpose-built for exactly this convergence. Its agentic AI layer (from Zive) connects to 20+ enterprise knowledge sources (Confluence, SharePoint, Slack, Teams, Google Drive, +15 more) to attack knowledge fragmentation directly; its skills intelligence (365Talents) rides the skills-based-organization shift; and its MCP Server makes Docebo data native inside Claude, Copilot and ChatGPT - meeting the agentic-AI-in-flow-of-work trend rather than fighting it. No legacy LMS (Cornerstone, SAP, Workday Learning), standalone KM tool (Notion, Guru, Bloomfire), or skills platform (Gloat, Eightfold) currently closes the full learning-knowledge-skills loop with agentic AI, so the macro shift opens a category-defining window for a credible, publicly-traded incumbent (Docebo, ~USD 249M ARR) with existing enterprise distribution.
findings.timeline: The category-definition window is open now and likely sharpest through roughly 2026-2028. Drivers: Gartner's 40%-of-apps-with-agents milestone lands in 2026, agentic-AI enterprise deployment expectations cluster in the next two years, and AgentHub's own agents go live fall 2026 with MCP Server GA in July 2026. Counter-pressure that narrows the window: Gartner forecasts >40% of agentic AI projects will be canceled by end of 2027 due to cost/value/risk concerns, and big-tech enterprise AI (Microsoft Copilot, Glean) plus incumbent LMS/HCM vendors are moving into the same space - so first-mover category framing and proof-of-value in 2026-2027 are critical before consolidation.

gaps: Time-bound quantification of how long the category window stays open is inferred from analyst forecasts (Gartner agent milestones, project-cancellation prediction) rather than a single authoritative timeline source.
Enterprise adoption figures for agentic AI specifically within L&D/HR (vs. enterprise-wide) are limited; most agentic-AI adoption data is cross-functional and applied here by extension.

metadata.scenario_emphasis_applied: new_product_launch: went deeper on TAM/growth by sizing all four relevant markets (corporate LMS/learning, enterprise knowledge management, skills intelligence/talent marketplace, and the emerging converged learning+knowledge+skills+agentic-AI category) with CAGRs and a discrepancy flag where sources diverged >30%; for Q8 went deeper on the macro trends (agentic AI enterprise adoption, knowledge fragmentation, skills-based organizations, AI in L&D, point-tool consolidation) with quantified momentum and enterprise adoption positioning.
